content.text: Throughput for an LLM serving system is measured in

— the total number of output tokens generated across all concurrent requests per second. Unlike latency, which is per-request, throughput is a system-level metric that determines cost efficiency: doubling throughput at the same hardware cost halves the cost per token.

attrs.alt: vLLM throughput optimisation: benchmark → profile → tune knobs → validate

content.text: The four-step loop is iterative. Each pass through identifies the binding constraint, applies the appropriate knobs, then re-benchmarks to confirm the gain and check for regressions.
Step 1: Benchmark with vLLM's Built-in Script
vLLM ships a standalone benchmark script that issues all prompts concurrently and measures end-to-end throughput. Run it against your target workload shape before touching any config:

# Clone vLLM if you don't have the benchmarks locally
git clone https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm.git --depth 1
cd vllm

# Basic throughput benchmark: 1000 prompts, 512 input tokens, 128 output tokens
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --num-prompts 1000 \
    --input-len 512 \
    --output-len 128 \
    --backend vllm

# Sample output:
# Throughput: 847.3 tokens/s
# Total time: 151.2s
# Successful requests: 1000

values that match your production workload. Chatbot workloads are often 200–800 tokens in, 50–200 tokens out. Document summarisation is 2000–8000 tokens in, 200–400 tokens out. Getting the shape right matters because throughput is dominated by the bottleneck phase (prefill or decode).

The throughput script issues all requests in parallel, which maximises batch size but does not model real arrival patterns. For a more realistic benchmark, use the serving benchmark that controls request rate:

# Start the vLLM server first
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --port 8000 &

# Then run the serving benchmark at 10 requests/second
python benchmarks/benchmark_serving.py \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --base-url http://localhost:8000 \
    --dataset-name random \
    --random-input-len 512 \
    --random-output-len 128 \
    --num-prompts 500 \
    --request-rate 10

# Output includes:
# Successful requests: 500
# Throughput: 9.97 req/s
# Mean TTFB: 0.312s    (time to first byte / prefill latency)
# P99 TTFB:  0.841s
# Mean TPOT: 0.042s    (time per output token / decode latency)
# P99 TPOT:  0.071s
Throughput is limited by exactly one of four constraints at any given load:
High TTFB, GPU compute at 100%, decode fast
Long prompts; compute-intensive attention over many tokens
Low GPU util (~10–30%), slow inter-token latency
Memory-bandwidth-bound: weight matrices reloaded per token
High preemption rate, swap activity, OOM
Too many concurrent sequences for available HBM
Long waiting queue, GPU not fully loaded
max_num_seqs too low, or serving capacity below traffic rate

# Check key metrics from vLLM Prometheus endpoint
curl -s http://localhost:8000/metrics | grep -E "vllm:(num_requests|gpu_cache|time)"

# Decode-bound signal: gpu_cache_usage low but TPOT high
# vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc 0.18        ← lots of free KV-cache
# vllm:time_per_output_token_seconds{q="0.5"} 0.065   ← slow decode

# KV-cache-bound signal: cache near full, preemptions happening
# vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc 0.97        ← nearly full
# vllm:num_preemptions_total 147        ← sequences being swapped

# Queue-bound signal: requests piling up but GPU not saturated
# vllm:num_requests_waiting 38
# vllm:num_requests_running 12          ← below max_num_seqs

When decode is the bottleneck, the GPU is underloaded — weight matrices are fetched from HBM for only a few active sequences. Increasing concurrency amortises the weight fetch across more sequences, improving compute intensity. The key knobs are:

# Allow more concurrent sequences — default 256, try 512+
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --max-num-seqs 512 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.92    # give more HBM to KV-cache to support 512 seqs

Long-prompt workloads cause prefill steps to take hundreds of milliseconds, stalling decode for all other sequences. Chunked prefill splits the prompt into chunks processed over multiple iterations, interleaved with decode steps. This caps TTFB variance at the cost of slightly lower peak prefill throughput:

# Enable chunked prefill — break long prompts into 512-token chunks
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --enable-chunked-prefill \
    --max-num-batched-tokens 2048    # total tokens per iteration (prefill + decode)

# Default: 90% of GPU HBM after weights reserved for KV-cache
# Increase carefully — leaving < 5% free risks CUDA OOM on spikes
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.95    # squeeze out more KV-cache space

# If OOM still occurs with long sequences, cap max sequence length
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --max-model-len 4096             # refuse requests > 4096 tokens
Quantisation reduces weight size, which directly reduces the memory-bandwidth cost of each decode step. It also frees HBM so more KV-cache fits, allowing more concurrent sequences:

# AWQ 4-bit: ~2× throughput on memory-bandwidth-bound workloads
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model TheBloke/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct-AWQ \
    --quantization awq \
    --max-num-seqs 512

# FP8 on H100 — lowest latency among quantised options
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --quantization fp8 \
    --kv-cache-dtype fp8
Step 4: Write a Throughput Regression Test
Encode the throughput target in a test that runs in CI before every deployment:

import subprocess, json, pytest

THROUGHPUT_FLOOR = 600    # tokens/s — fail if we drop below this
MODEL = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"

@pytest.mark.slow
def test_throughput_meets_floor():
    """Offline throughput benchmark — must exceed THROUGHPUT_FLOOR tok/s."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        [
            "python", "benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py",
            "--model", MODEL,
            "--num-prompts", "200",
            "--input-len", "512",
            "--output-len", "128",
            "--backend", "vllm",
            "--output-json", "/tmp/bench.json",
        ],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        timeout=300,
    )
    assert result.returncode == 0, result.stderr

    with open("/tmp/bench.json") as f:
        data = json.load(f)

    throughput = data["throughput"]   # tokens/s
    assert throughput >= THROUGHPUT_FLOOR, (
        f"Throughput {throughput:.1f} tok/s is below floor {THROUGHPUT_FLOOR} tok/s"
    )

Observed throughput varies widely with model size, hardware, and quantisation. The table below shows representative values from the vLLM benchmark suite (A100 80 GB SXM, batch of 1000 prompts, 512 in / 128 out):

These numbers are indicative; your workload shape (especially output length) has a large effect. Decode-heavy workloads (many output tokens) benefit most from quantisation. Prefill-heavy workloads (large inputs, few outputs) benefit more from tensor parallelism and chunked prefill.

When requests share a common prefix (system prompt, retrieval context), prefix caching eliminates the prefill cost for those tokens on subsequent requests. The effect on throughput depends on prefix hit rate:

# Estimate prefix cache benefit
def estimate_prefix_cache_speedup(
    prompt_len: int,
    shared_prefix_len: int,
    hit_rate: float,           # fraction of requests hitting the prefix cache
    prefill_tps: float = 5000, # tokens/s prefill speed (compute-bound)
    decode_tps: float = 850,   # tokens/s decode speed
    output_len: int = 128,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compute expected throughput speedup from prefix caching.
    """
    unique_prefix_len = prompt_len - shared_prefix_len

    # Without caching: always compute full prompt prefill
    base_prefill_time = prompt_len / prefill_tps
    base_decode_time = output_len / decode_tps
    base_total = base_prefill_time + base_decode_time

    # With caching: cached hits skip the shared prefix prefill
    cached_prefill_time = unique_prefix_len / prefill_tps
    cached_total = cached_prefill_time + base_decode_time

    effective_total = (1 - hit_rate) * base_total + hit_rate * cached_total
    speedup = base_total / effective_total

    return {
        "base_latency_s": round(base_total, 4),
        "cached_hit_latency_s": round(cached_total, 4),
        "effective_latency_s": round(effective_total, 4),
        "throughput_speedup": round(speedup, 2),
    }


# 80% of requests hit a 1024-token system-prompt prefix
result = estimate_prefix_cache_speedup(
    prompt_len=1536,
    shared_prefix_len=1024,
    hit_rate=0.80,
    output_len=200,
)
print(result)
# {'base_latency_s': 0.5418, 'cached_hit_latency_s': 0.3361,
#  'effective_latency_s': 0.3773, 'throughput_speedup': 1.44}
Tensor Parallelism and Its Effect on Throughput
Tensor parallelism (TP) shards each attention and FFN weight matrix across N GPUs so that every forward pass involves all N GPUs simultaneously. The effect on throughput has two sides:

aggregate HBM bandwidth scales linearly with N — 4×A100 provides 4× the memory bandwidth, which directly reduces memory-bandwidth-bound decode latency. More HBM also allows more KV-cache, enabling higher concurrency.

every layer requires an all-reduce across N GPUs, adding latency proportional to model width. On NVLink this overhead is small (~0.5 ms per layer for Llama-70B with TP=4). Over PCIe or Ethernet it can dominate.

use the minimum TP that fits the model in VRAM, then tune batch size and quantisation for throughput. Adding more GPUs with TP is expensive; quantisation gives similar throughput gains at zero hardware cost.

# Measure throughput at TP=1 vs TP=2 on 2×A100
# TP=1 (one GPU)
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --num-prompts 500 --input-len 512 --output-len 128 \
    --tensor-parallel-size 1

# TP=2 (two GPUs) — decode-bound: expect ~1.8× throughput
python benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --num-prompts 500 --input-len 512 --output-len 128 \
    --tensor-parallel-size 2

# Prefill-bound: TP=2 gives closer to 2× since compute also scales
# Decode-bound: gains are sub-linear at high TP due to all-reduce overhead

Values above 0.95 leave insufficient headroom for PyTorch activation memory, CUDA kernel workspace, and NCCL communication buffers. The result is a CUDA OOM that kills the server process — not a graceful degradation. Start at 0.90, raise by 0.01 increments, and load-test before moving to production.

prompts. Real workloads have high variance — some requests are 50 tokens, others 4000. High-variance workloads stress the scheduler more (small sequences pad large batches, reducing GPU efficiency). Always validate with a sample from real traffic before committing to a config.

import random, statistics

def sample_production_lengths(n: int = 1000) -> list[tuple[int, int]]:
    """
    Generate synthetic length distribution approximating a chatbot workload.
    Returns list of (input_len, output_len) pairs.
    """
    pairs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        # Input: mix of short queries and long context
        if random.random() < 0.7:
            input_len = int(random.lognormvariate(5.0, 0.8))   # ~150 median
        else:
            input_len = int(random.lognormvariate(7.0, 0.6))   # ~1100 median
        # Output: usually shorter than input
        output_len = int(random.lognormvariate(4.2, 0.9))      # ~66 median
        pairs.append((max(10, min(input_len, 4096)), max(10, min(output_len, 512))))
    return pairs

pairs = sample_production_lengths()
inputs = [p[0] for p in pairs]
outputs = [p[1] for p in pairs]
print(f"Input  P50={statistics.median(inputs):.0f}  P99={sorted(inputs)[990]}")
print(f"Output P50={statistics.median(outputs):.0f} P99={sorted(outputs)[990]}")
Ignoring TTFB When Optimising for Throughput

raises throughput but also increases prefill queuing time (TTFB), because each new request must wait for all previously queued prefills to complete before its own prefill runs. In a user-facing chatbot, TTFB > 1s is noticeable. Set a per-request timeout at the load balancer (e.g. 2s TTFB SLA) and test that max-num-seqs tuning doesn't breach it before optimising purely for throughput.

When KV-cache is full and a high-priority request arrives, vLLM can either swap a running sequence's KV blocks to CPU DRAM or discard and recompute them later. The default is swap. Swapping keeps decode progress but adds PCIe transfer overhead (~10 GB/s on PCIe 4.0). Recompute discards the KV-cache and re-runs prefill on resumption — fast for short sequences but expensive for long ones. Set the policy via:

# Use recompute instead of swap (better for short prompts, saves CPU DRAM)
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --preemption-mode recompute
Every throughput optimisation has a latency implication. The table below summarises the key knobs and their trade-offs to help choose the right setting for a given SLA:
Batch/async workloads; chatbot with loose TTFB SLA
Almost always worth it for decode-heavy loads
When model doesn't fit in 1 GPU VRAM

Throughput tuning for vLLM follows a tight loop: benchmark with realistic workload shapes, profile to find the binding constraint (prefill, decode, KV-cache, or queue), apply targeted knobs (chunked prefill, batch size, memory utilisation, quantisation), then re-benchmark to confirm the gain. Quantisation (AWQ or FP8) is the single highest-leverage knob for decode-heavy workloads — it directly reduces memory bandwidth pressure and frees HBM for more concurrent sequences. Prefix caching adds a multiplicative speedup on top of that for workloads with shared prefixes. Encode a throughput floor as a CI regression test so configuration changes that degrade serving throughput are caught before they reach production.
